paper_summary.main_contribution: The paper summarizes the author's research career in primatology, focusing on the socio-sexual behaviors and social dynamics of various primate species, including Japanese macaques and ringtailed lemurs, with a particular emphasis on understanding these behaviors through an evolutionary lens.
paper_summary.narrative: The paper begins with observations of Japanese macaques, highlighting unexpected mating behaviors such as high-ranking males not obtaining high mating success (Fig. 1). The author then explores long-lasting nonsexual male-female relationships, illustrated by the Peculiar-Proximate Relation (PPR) between an alpha male and a female (Fig. 2). The research extends to other primate species, including chimpanzees and ringtailed lemurs, with observations of social interactions and competitive behaviors (Fig. 7). The narrative is supported by fieldwork and collaborative research efforts, as shown by the campsite image (Fig. 6).
paper_summary.limitations_noted: The paper relies heavily on qualitative observations and lacks statistical data to support some of the behavioral claims.

items.label: Figure 1
items.caption: Fig. 1 The  alpha  male  (M-59 ♂ )  copulating  with  a  high-ranking female (Ch-67 ♀ )
